beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I need to unlearn the photon-kick story and see the Robertson bound as a property of the wavefunction itself. The distinction from Ozawa's measurement-disturbance result is the part I keep blurring. Walk me through: (1) deriving sigma-x times sigma-p at least h-bar over 2 from the commutator being i-h-bar, with no apparatus in the argument, (2) computing both widths for the Gaussian ground state to show it saturates the bound, and (3) stating precisely how Robertson 1929 differs from Ozawa 2003 so I stop conflating them.

props.command: I need to unlearn the photon-kick story and see the Robertson bound as a property of the wavefunction itself. The distinction from Ozawa's measurement-disturbance result is the part I keep blurring. Walk me through: (1) deriving sigma-x times sigma-p at least h-bar over 2 from the commutator being i-h-bar, with no apparatus in the argument, (2) computing both widths for the Gaussian ground state to show it saturates the bound, and (3) stating precisely how Robertson 1929 differs from Ozawa 2003 so I stop conflating them.

beats.narration_text: You've heard the story. To see an electron you need a photon. That photon kicks the electron and smears its momentum.

card.headline: Why Uncertainty Isn't About Bumping the Particle

beats.narration_text: The balloon analogy. Bump it to find it, bumping moves it. Seems right. Gives the right order-of-magnitude. But it is the wrong picture.
beats.description: CRIMSON balloon with photon arrow bouncing off it. Red X overlay. Text: 'measurement disturbance — Heisenberg 1927 intuition'

beats.narration_text: The question: if the uncertainty principle isn't about the kick, what is it actually about?

card.headline: What is the uncertainty principle actually about?

beats.narration_text: Here is what Robertson actually proved in 1929. A Gaussian state has width σ_x in position and width σ_p in momentum. Squeeze one — the other must grow.
beats.description: Two bar-gauges: σ_x (TEAL, left) and σ_p (TEAL, right). As σ_x bar shrinks, σ_p bar grows. No photon, no apparatus. Label: 'Before any measurement'

beats.narration_text: The Robertson bound: σ_x times σ_p is always at least ℏ over 2. This is a property of the wavefunction itself — no apparatus, no kick, no observer.
beats.description: Equation: σ_x · σ_p ≥ ℏ/2 in TEAL, centered. Below: two Gaussian probability curves side by side — narrow position / wide momentum vs wide position / narrow momentum

card.headline: Non-commuting operators bound the state itself.

beats.narration_text: Position and momentum don't commute: x-hat times p-hat minus p-hat times x-hat equals i-ℏ. That commutator is why the two widths can't both be zero simultaneously.
beats.description: Equation: [x̂, p̂] = iℏ in TEAL. Below: 'Commutator ≠ 0 → joint floor on σ_x · σ_p'. Two operators shown as colored boxes that don't line up when swapped.

beats.narration_text: The harmonic oscillator ground state saturates the bound exactly: σ_x times σ_p equals ℏ over 2. Every excited state exceeds it. The ground state is the tightest allowed configuration.
beats.description: Energy-level diagram of harmonic oscillator. Ground state n=0 labeled 'saturates: σ_x·σ_p = ℏ/2'. n=1,2,3 shown above with 'σ_x·σ_p = (n+½)ℏ > ℏ/2'. TEAL horizontal bars.

card.headline: Uncertainty is in the state. No apparatus required.

beats.narration_text: This is why you cannot even prepare a particle with simultaneously sharp position and momentum. Not measurement, not disturbance — preparation. The state forbids it before you look.

props.headline: This is why you cannot even prepare a particle with simultaneously…

beats.still_prompt: B10, clean physics diagram: two side-by-side Gaussian probability distributions. LEFT labeled 'narrow σ_x' shows a sharp tall teal bell curve in position space. RIGHT labeled 'wide σ_p' shows a broad flat teal bell curve in momentum space. At the bottom, equation: σ_x · σ_p ≥ ℏ/2 in teal. Black background, white labels, clean educational style, no apparatus, no photon, no observer.

beats.narration_text: Two different inequalities, often confused. Robertson 1929: preparation uncertainty — the state itself. Ozawa 2003: measurement-disturbance — apparatus noise plus recoil. Both real, not the same.
beats.description: Side-by-side comparison table. Left: 'Robertson 1929 — Preparation' with σ_x·σ_p ≥ ℏ/2, TEAL box. Right: 'Ozawa 2003 — Measurement-Disturbance', CRIMSON box. Dividing line in center.

beats.narration_text: Ground state ψ₀ proportional to e to the minus alpha x-squared over 2. Computing directly: σ_x equals root ℏ over 2m-omega; σ_p equals root ℏ-m-omega over 2. Their product is exactly ℏ over 2. Equality — the floor is saturated.
beats.description: Step-by-step: ψ₀ ∝ e^{-αx²/2}, α = mω/ℏ. Then σ_x² = ℏ/(2mω), σ_p² = ℏmω/2. Then σ_x·σ_p = ℏ/2. Each line TEAL. Final result boxed in TEAL: 'Equality: minimum-uncertainty state'.

beats.narration_text: The question was: what is the uncertainty principle actually about? The answer: it is a lower bound on the product of widths of any quantum state — written before any measurement, from nothing but non-commuting algebra.

card.headline:
The state itself is uncertain.
No kick required.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Ground state ψ₀ proportional to e to the minus alpha x-squared over 2. Computing directly: σ_x equals root ℏ over 2m-omega; σ_p equals root ℏ-m-omega over 2. Their product is exactly ℏ over 2. Equality — the floor is saturated. The question was: what is the uncertainty principle actually about? The answer: it is a lower bound on the product of widths of any quantum state — written before any measurement, from nothing but non-commuting algebra.

artifactLines: Ground state ψ₀ proportional to e to the minus alpha x-squared over 2.
The question was: what is the uncertainty principle actually about?

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [the mechanism just described] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?

props.subline: the answer: it is a lower bound on the product of widths of any quantum state —
